rationale_for_baseline_tier: A known HCV cirrhotic with a sudden severe tearing RUQ pain (suspected intratumoural haemorrhage / capsular rupture), rapidly worsening tense ascites causing orthopnoea and tachypnoea (respiratory compromise), and patient-reportable racing heart/weakness pointing to early haemodynamic compromise. The source clinician mandated immediate ambulance transfer. These are patient-reportable, time-critical features — an acute abdomen with respiratory and possible haemorrhagic compromise — so T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) is the honest tier. This is NOT a fear-inflated stable-cancer work-up (which would ceiling at T3): the acuity is real and independently drove an ambulance dispatch.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe tearing abdominal pain with a racing heart, weakness, or feeling faint (possible internal bleeding)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — call 000 immediately; do not wait
escalation_edges.rationale: Bleeding into or rupture of a hypervascular liver tumour can cause rapid haemorrhagic shock; emergency imaging and intervention are time-critical.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because you have severe tearing pain and your heart is racing and you feel weak, call 000 for an ambulance right now — this could be internal bleeding and needs the hospital immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening breathlessness or inability to lie flat from the tense abdominal swelling
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — stay upright and keep 000 on the line if breathing worsens
escalation_edges.rationale: Tense ascites splinting the diaphragm can cause progressive respiratory failure; urgent decompression is a hospital procedure.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Stay sitting bolt upright. If your breathing gets worse while you wait, tell 000 straight away — they can prioritise the ambulance.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New jaundice with worsening abdominal pain and swelling in a known cirrhotic
escalation_edges.rationale: New jaundice on a background of cirrhosis with acute pain marks hepatic decompensation that needs urgent in-hospital assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: The new yellowing of your eyes together with this pain and swelling means your liver is under serious strain — this needs to be seen at hospital tonight.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting blood, black tarry stools, new confusion or drowsiness, or collapse
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 and tell the call-taker what is happening
escalation_edges.rationale: Variceal haemorrhage and hepatic encephalopathy are life-threatening cirrhosis complications; their appearance independently mandates emergency response.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he vomits blood, passes black stools, becomes confused or drowsy, or collapses, call 000 immediately and tell them exactly what is happening.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Spouse present and highly distressed by the rapid decline and pain; is the person who made contact
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give the spouse clear, calm, step-by-step instructions: call 000, keep him upright, unlock the door for paramedics, watch for the escalation warning signs, and do not give any further medication or fluids.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Known cirrhosis with existing ascites and diuretics — high baseline risk of variceal bleeding, SBP and encephalopathy
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Treat any acute change as a decompensation emergency rather than a routine fluctuation; the escalation threshold is deliberately low.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now — this is an emergency. Tell them he has severe liver pain, a rapidly swelling abdomen, new jaundice, is breathless and cannot lie flat, and has a known scarred liver (cirrhosis). Keep him sitting bolt upright, do not let him lie flat or walk around, do not give any more water tablets, pain medicine or fluids, and unlock the door for the paramedics. Stay with him and watch for any vomiting of blood, black stools, confusion, drowsiness or collapse.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this cannot wait for morning or a routine GP appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary with the imaging, paracentesis and blood results and the hepatology/oncology plan — take these to the GP and liver specialist for ongoing management.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the acute decompensation as a chronic-ascites nuisance and routing to home diuretic management, telehealth follow-up or a routine/urgent GP appointment.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An unrecognised intra-abdominal haemorrhage or progressive respiratory compromise deteriorates to shock or respiratory failure at home while awaiting a non-emergency review.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising self-transport to ED rather than a 000 ambulance despite respiratory compromise and possible bleeding — deterioration en route without paramedic monitoring or resuscitation capability.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Over-triage above T5 is not possible; T5 is the correct and maximal tier for this presentation.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the acute deterioration is intratumoural haemorrhage/rupture, malignant portal-vein thrombosis, tense ascites alone, or SBP — none distinguishable via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate to 000/ED for definitive imaging and paracentesis; the uncertainty itself is a reason to escalate, not to temporise.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Attempting to differentiate the cause remotely, adjusting diuretics 'to see if it settles', or naming/treating the cancer before hospital assessment.
